metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether fsck_recover.py should acquire `write_lock` (and thus be covered by the gate at choke point 1) or be gated explicitly; the current code has no `write_lock` call, making the plan's coverage claim false — update the plan to either (a) add write_lock acquisition to fsck_recover.py's mutation operations and gate there, or (b) enumerate fsck_recover as a third choke point requiring an explicit gate call.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'fsck_recover.py acquires write_lock, so it is covered by choke point (1) — gated when the record is incompatible (it mutates store state)'. Grep of src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py finds zero write_lock or lock.acquire calls. The file uses raw git subprocess calls (git cherry-pick, git rebase --continue, git merge --abort) via its _git() helper which calls run_git() directly with no locking. This means fsck_recover.py is NOT structurally covered by placing the gate inside write_lock acquisition — it is an un-gated mutating path that the plan incorrectly scopes out as 'already covered'.
findings.location: What section — 'fsck_recover.py acquires write_lock, so it is covered by choke point (1)' / src/rebar/_commands/fsck_recover.py
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `with write_lock(tracker_dir): ...` around the cherry-pick/rebase mutation block in fsck_recover.py (which also fixes a real concurrency gap) so the gate inside write_lock covers it, or add an explicit `check_store_compat(tracker_dir)` call at the top of fsck_recover_cli before any mutation — and update the plan's 'two choke points' claim to reflect the actual count.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the gate is placed in `lock.acquire()` (not in the `write_lock()` wrapper body), so that the four existing direct-acquire callers (`compact.py:167,415`, `txn.py:85`, `fsck.py:419`) and any future `acquire()` callers are covered; update the CI lint grep to target `acquire(` not just `write_lock(`.
findings.finding: The plan says to gate 'inside `write_lock` acquisition' but the codebase has callers that bypass `write_lock()` entirely and call `lock.acquire()` directly: `compact.py:167`, `compact.py:415`, `txn.py:85`, `fsck.py:419`. Placing the gate only in the `write_lock()` context manager body leaves these four mutation paths structurally ungated, defeating the 'no enumeration required' invariant the plan asserts.
findings.location: What section — 'Gate at the structural choke point'; also src/rebar/_store/lock.py:307 (write_lock) vs src/rebar/_commands/compact.py:167,415, src/rebar/_commands/txn.py:85, src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:419
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `StoreIncompatibleError` check into `lock.acquire()` immediately after `canonical_tracker()` resolves the path and before the fcntl/mkdir legs are attempted. Since `write_lock()` delegates entirely to `acquire()`, all callers — context-manager and manual-handle — are covered by a single insertion point with no enumeration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the gate should be placed at `lock.acquire()` (the common floor used by both `write_lock()` and `txn.py`'s `_acquire_write_lock`) rather than inside `write_lock()` alone, so that `txn.py`'s direct `lock.acquire()` calls for claim/transition are also structurally covered.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'there are exactly two choke points every side-effecting path already funnels through' and that placing the gate 'inside write_lock acquisition' structurally covers all store mutations — but src/rebar/_commands/txn.py acquires the lock via lock.acquire() directly (through _acquire_write_lock), not through the write_lock() context manager. If the gate is implemented as a shim only around write_lock(), the claim and transition code paths in txn.py are structurally excluded, falsifying the 'no enumeration' guarantee.
findings.location: What section — 'Gate at the structural choke point' / choke point 1; src/rebar/_commands/txn.py lines 79–87 (_acquire_write_lock calls lock.acquire() not write_lock())
findings.suggested_fix: Place the capability gate inside `lock.acquire()` (in `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`) rather than as a wrapper around `write_lock()`, since `acquire()` is the single convergence point that both `write_lock()` and `txn.py`'s `_acquire_write_lock` call. Update the plan's choke-point description to name `acquire()` specifically. The CI lint should then grep for `lock.acquire(` and `write_lock(` (both forms) and assert gate coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the gate intercepts at `write_lock()` or `lock.acquire()`, and if at `write_lock()`, enumerate the known direct-`acquire()` callers (`compact.py:167`, `compact.py:415`, `fsck.py:419`, `txn.py:85`) as sites that must also be updated — or move the gate into `acquire()` itself so it is universal.
findings.finding: The plan claims the gate lives 'inside write_lock acquisition' as a structural choke point covering all store mutations, but the codebase has three proven call sites that call `lock.acquire()` directly, bypassing the `write_lock()` wrapper: `compact.py:167`, `compact.py:415`, and `fsck.py:419`. If the gate is inserted inside `write_lock()`, these callers are ungated. The plan provides no fallback specification (enumeration, lint rule, or secondary intercept) for direct-acquire callers.
findings.location: What section — 'Gate at the structural choke point'; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Move the gate check into `lock.acquire()` rather than `write_lock()`, so every code path that holds a write lock — whether via the contextmanager or the raw acquire — is covered without enumeration. Update the AC3 lint/grep to search for `lock.acquire(` in addition to `write_lock(`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve 'Confirm which case holds' in the plan itself: state whether pre-v1.0 binaries ignore or error on an unknown store file, and either remove the compensating-delete note (if ignore) or promote it to a tested AC (if error) — do not leave it as an open question for the executor.
findings.finding: The Rollback Safety section ends with 'Confirm which case holds and document it' — this is a literal design-decision placeholder that defers to the executor whether pre-v1.0 binaries would parse-and-error on the record or silently ignore it. The answer determines whether the delete-compensating path is a documentation note or a hard requirement verified by a test, so the implementation differs materially depending on the answer.
findings.location: What — 'Rollback safety (concrete).' bullet, final sentence: 'Confirm which case holds and document it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve, in the plan, which pre-v1.0 binary behavior holds (ignored-unknown or parse-and-error) by inspecting the existing ensure/compaction/fsck code paths, state the conclusion explicitly, and update AC5 to assert the specific expected outcome of the rollback test (operable without delete, or operable after delete).
findings.finding: The plan leaves the pre-v1.0 binary behavior toward `.store-compat.json` as an unresolved open item ('Confirm which case holds and document it'), deferring a material design decision to implementation; the rollback safety guarantee and the delete-compensating-path documentation depend on this answer being established before code is written.
findings.location: What → 'Rollback safety (concrete)' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Record update' subsection specifying: (a) the code path that updates an already-present record when a new capability ships, (b) that the update is performed atomically (tempfile+rename) under the write_lock, and (c) that the operation is idempotent (check-then-write inside the lock, not outside it).
findings.finding: The shared-state UPDATE lifecycle for `.store-compat.json` is unspecified: the plan defines CREATE (ensure unit writes when absent) but gives no owner, mechanism, write path, or concurrency controls for future updates — e.g. when a new capability is added to `required_capabilities` in a future release.
findings.location: What → 'Recognised-capability vocabulary' and 'Expand phase' sections; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and document the concrete post-rollback schema state: confirm (via an explicit test against the oldest supported pre-v1.0 binary) whether `.store-compat.json` is silently ignored or causes an error, commit that result as a fact in `docs/migrations.md`, and — if the file causes an error — promote the delete-`.store-compat.json` compensating path from 'documented' to 'automated or operationally scripted' with a `DATA LOSS RISK` note if any writes occurred between record creation and rollback.
findings.finding: The post-rollback schema state is explicitly unresolved: the plan says 'Confirm which case holds and document it' but never commits to an assertion about whether a pre-v1.0 binary silently ignores `.store-compat.json` or errors on it. If a pre-v1.0 binary errors on the file, rolling back the binary code leaves the store bricked until the operator manually deletes the record — a data-availability risk that the plan treats as only conditional and deferred rather than definitively answered.
findings.location: What section — 'Rollback safety (concrete)' paragraph; AC line 5 (rollback test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete sentence to the plan resolving the conditional: run the existing pre-v1.0 ensure units (which touch only `.gitignore`/`.gitattributes`/`.env-id`/etc.) against a store containing `.store-compat.json` and assert in a test that the old code does NOT touch or parse the file. If confirmed safe, change 'Confirm which case holds' to 'Confirmed: pre-v1.0 binaries do not parse `.store-compat.json`; no compensating delete is needed on rollback.' If not safe, add an automated `fsck --repair` step to the rollback runbook that removes the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the open question in the What section ('Confirm which case holds') **before** the AC is finalized: either (a) confirm the pre-v1.0 binary ignores .store-compat.json and remove the conditional language, updating AC5 to say 'ignored-unknown guarantee is verified by the rollback test,' or (b) if the pre-v1.0 binary parses it, update AC5 to drop the 'ignored-unknown guarantee' framing and instead verify the delete-compensating-path explicitly.
findings.finding: The What section contains an unresolved open question — 'Confirm which case holds and document it' — about whether a pre-v1.0 binary parses-and-errors on .store-compat.json, making the rollback path conditional. AC5 simultaneously asserts the 'ignored-unknown guarantee' as an already-established hard requirement to verify. The sections contradict: What treats the case as open; AC5 treats it as resolved.
findings.location: What section ('Rollback safety (concrete)') vs AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'Confirm which case holds and document it' with a definitive statement (based on reading old ensure/fsck/compact code paths). If confirmed safe-to-ignore, AC5 stands as is. If not, update AC5 to: 'A rollback test simulates a pre-v1.0 binary against a store carrying the committed record; if the binary parses it, the test verifies the delete-.store-compat.json compensating path restores operability and documents this in docs/migrations.md.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific function/boundary that constitutes the 'one push entry point' for the Jira gate (e.g., `applier.apply()` outbound-direction dispatch, or the `_apply_typed`/`dispatch_mutation` boundary for outbound leaves only), and clarify how it avoids blocking inbound-direction Jira reads that legitimately occur in the same pass.
findings.finding: The plan designates 'immediately before the outbound Jira push call at the one push entry point' as the second choke point, but the actual Jira write path is not a single thin call-site: `reconcile_once` → `applier.apply()` → `_apply_mutations` → `apply_outbound` leaf handlers → `batch_dispatch._call_with_retry` → `acli_cli_ops` REST calls. There is no single function that represents 'the outbound Jira call'; the plan does not identify which specific function or boundary is the intended gate location, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.location: What section — 'Outbound Jira publish → the single reconciler push call-site'; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py:847-858, applier.py, apply_outbound.py, batch_dispatch.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'e.g. `.store-compat.json`' with the definitive committed path (e.g. state 'the path is `.store-compat.json`') so all four coordinated sites (writer, gate, rollback test fixture, docs) have a single authoritative reference.
findings.finding: The record file path is stated only as 'e.g. `.store-compat.json`' — 'e.g.' notation leaves the actual path as a defaultable executor decision rather than a committed specification, which could produce inconsistency between the ensure writer, the gate reader, the rollback test, and documentation.
findings.location: What — 'The record.' bullet: 'a fixed store path (e.g. `.store-compat.json`)'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'The record is written to and read from `.store-compat.json` at the store root (committed to the `tickets` branch). This path is the canonical reference for the ensure writer, the gate, rollback tests, and docs.' Remove 'e.g.'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and enforce in the AC) that the compat-file read occurs after the write lock is held (not before), or explicitly acknowledge the TOCTOU window is accepted as negligible and state the rationale.
findings.finding: The plan places the gate 'inside write_lock acquisition' but does not clarify whether the compat-file read occurs while the write lock is already held or before it is acquired; if the gate reads the file before the lock is held, there is a TOCTOU window where the record can be replaced between the gate check and the lock grant.
findings.location: What → 'Gate at the structural choke point' section, choke point 1
findings.suggested_fix: State in the 'Gate at the structural choke point' section: 'The gate reads `.store-compat.json` as the first action inside the write_lock context (after the lock fd is obtained), not before acquisition, eliminating the check-then-act window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the compat gate inside `acquire()` should apply to the push-retry merge path in `push.py:186`, and if so, confirm that raising `StoreIncompatibleError` there (after a successful commit) is acceptable UX; if not, specify the exemption mechanism (e.g., a `skip_compat_check` kwarg to `acquire()`).
findings.finding: The plan places the gate inside `write_lock` / `acquire`, but `push.py:186` also acquires `write_lock` for the non-fast-forward push-retry merge — a best-effort, post-commit operation. Under the proposal, an incompatible store would block even the best-effort push-retry merge, stranding the already-committed local data as PUSH_PENDING indefinitely and making the push retry path loudly fail on stores that are 'incompatible' but whose local data is already committed. The plan does not address this case.
findings.location: What section — gate at write_lock; src/rebar/_store/push.py:186

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the docs AC, e.g. `grep -qE 'format_version' docs/migrations.md && grep -qE 'required_capabilities' docs/migrations.md && grep -qE 'rollback' docs/migrations.md` (or a docs-linting CI step), so the criterion is machine-verifiable rather than requiring manual review.
findings.finding: The docs AC ('docs/migrations.md documents the record, the four states, the fail-closed gate, the deployment-order rationale, and the concrete rollback state') has no concrete proving command — no grep, jq, or CI check is named — making it unverifiable in-session beyond a manual read.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — seventh bullet (docs): 'docs/migrations.md documents the record...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1) that verifies the `_version.py` deliverable: e.g., 'SCHEMA_VERSION and format_version are documented as independent discriminators in `_version.py` (code comment or docstring), and a test or lint asserts that `SCHEMA_VERSION` is not compared in the fail-closed gate.'
findings.finding: The Scope section lists `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` as a deliverable ('relate format_version to the existing preserve-and-ignore KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES machinery and to SCHEMA_VERSION — state whether they move together or independently') but no AC verifies this relationship or the clarification it produces.
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/reducer/_version.py') and AC list (all 8 items)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or `CLAUDE.md` section documenting the git-style format-version gate pattern: the two choke points, how to wire a new mutation path through the gate, the `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES` lifecycle (when and how to add a new capability string), and the relationship between `format_version` and `SCHEMA_VERSION` — so contributors have authoritative guidance before the CI lint catches a violation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant architectural pattern — a mandatory gate that ALL current and future store-mutation paths must pass through, with a frozenset capability vocabulary and a two-choke-point enforcement model — but scopes its documentation only to `docs/migrations.md` (operator/user-facing) and code comments. No ADR or `CLAUDE.md` update is planned. Contributors adding a new write path need to understand the gate requirement BEFORE writing code; the CI grep-lint provides a safety net but not the rationale or how-to.
findings.location: Scope section and What section ('Gate at the structural choke point'); AC line 3 (coverage test/lint); no mention of ADR or CLAUDE.md in Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'CLAUDE.md (or docs/adr/NNN-store-compat-gate.md) — document the gate pattern, two choke points, capability-vocabulary lifecycle, and contributor checklist for new write/side-effect paths.' The ADR can be short (1–2 pages) and cross-referenced from docs/migrations.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC3 sub-case requiring a test that writes a record with `format_version` set to an unrecognised integer (e.g. `{"format_version": 999, "required_capabilities": []}`) and asserts the gate raises `StoreIncompatibleError` — independently of the `required_capabilities` path — so the format_version check cannot be omitted and go undetected.
findings.finding: AC3's verification test only drives the required_capabilities incompatibility path (e.g. `["unknown-v99"]`) and never tests format_version-alone incompatibility (e.g. format_version=999 with empty required_capabilities). An implementation that checks required_capabilities but never validates format_version would satisfy every AC while silently passing incompatible stores with an unrecognised format_version.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 / What — Four record states (3)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, extend the test description to: '(a) a record with required_capabilities=["unknown-v99"] and format_version=1 (tests the capability path), (b) a record with format_version=999 and required_capabilities=[] (tests the format_version path independently), asserting StoreIncompatibleError is raised in both cases and store files are byte-unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact grep/AST pattern for the write_lock lint AC (e.g., `grep -rn --include='*.py' -E 'write_lock\s*\('`) and state how 'passes through the gate' is asserted (e.g., the surrounding N lines contain an import of `check_store_compat` or a call to the gate function), so the lint cannot silently miss new acquisition sites.
findings.finding: The AC for the grep-based write_lock lint check (AC3b) describes the intent ('greps the tree for every `write_lock(` acquisition site and asserts each passes through the gate') but does not specify the grep pattern or assertion form, risking substring false-positives or false-negatives (e.g., `write_lock` vs. `write_lock(` vs. `acquire_write_lock(`)  that could silently miss un-gated call sites.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet (gate choke points), sub-item (b): 'a coverage test/lint that greps the tree for every `write_lock(` acquisition site'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rollback simulation mechanism in the AC (e.g., 'use a test helper that imports the store module stack WITHOUT importing the compat-gate module, exercising the same code paths a pre-v1.0 binary would take') so the test proves actual old-binary behaviour rather than a weakened proxy.
findings.finding: The rollback AC requires a test that 'simulates a pre-v1.0 binary — the store layer invoked with the gate code path absent' but specifies no mechanism for the simulation (monkey-patching, a separate test helper that imports the store without the gate module, a feature-flag, etc.). An executor cannot write this test without deciding the simulation approach, which is a non-trivial design choice with different tradeoffs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (rollback test): 'simulates a pre-v1.0 binary — the store layer invoked with the gate code path absent'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Scope section to name the specific files/entry points for wiring the ensure unit at fsck-repair (e.g., `_commands/fsck.py` repair path) and MCP-boot (e.g., the MCP server boot entry point), matching the three trigger points asserted in AC2.
findings.finding: The Scope section names only `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` as the location for the ensure unit that writes the compatibility record, but AC2 requires the ensure unit to run at 'init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot' — the implementation locations for fsck-repair and MCP-boot entry points are not named in the Scope, leaving those wiring points uncovered by the decomposition.
findings.location: Scope ('src/rebar/_commands/init.py — the ensure unit') vs AC2 ('init / fsck-repair / MCP-boot')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structured log line (at WARNING or ERROR level) and, if the project has a metrics layer, a counter increment inside the gate when `StoreIncompatibleError` is raised, and document both in the AC so operators can monitor gate-fire events.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation (log line, metric counter, or alert) for gate-fire events — neither blocked-and-raised nor allowed-through — so operators cannot monitor gate trigger rates, detect repeated incompatibility blocks in production, or set alerts when a binary encounters an unknown capability.
findings.location: What → 'The gate error' section; Acceptance Criteria item 3
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate function, after deciding to raise `StoreIncompatibleError`, emit `logger.warning('store-compat gate blocked: version=%s unsupported_capabilities=%s path=%s', format_version, unsupported, record_path)` before raising. Add a corresponding AC bullet: 'a test asserts a WARNING-level log line is emitted when the gate fires, containing the capability/version identifier.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document the capability string vocabulary (syntax, naming convention, namespace rules) in the new module's docstring or in `docs/migrations.md`, so future implementers adding the first real capability have a normative format to follow.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES: frozenset[str]` but never defines the capability string vocabulary — its syntax, naming convention, or who assigns capability identifiers. Future implementers adding the first real capability have no specified format (e.g. whether strings are kebab-case, namespaced, follow a schema like 'feature-v1', or are arbitrary). This is a referenced enum whose value vocabulary is never defined.
findings.location: What section — 'Recognised-capability vocabulary'; Scope — 'New module under src/rebar/_store/'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring to the new module specifying the capability string format, e.g.: 'Capability strings are lowercase kebab-case ASCII, e.g. "signed-keyring-v1". They are registered in this module when the consuming feature ships. A string added to a store record but absent from KNOWN_CAPABILITIES in the reading binary causes fail-closed.' Also add a validation assertion in the gate that capability strings match the naming regex.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `CURRENT_FORMAT_VERSION: int = 1` (or equivalent) to the new module's exported surface, and specify in the gate's decision rule that `format_version > CURRENT_FORMAT_VERSION` (or not in a known set) triggers fail-closed — making the version comparison rule concrete and machine-checkable.
findings.finding: The plan never exports or names a `CURRENT_FORMAT_VERSION` (or equivalent) constant from the new module — only the record body example contains `format_version: 1`. The gate's decision rule for `format_version` is 'unknown format_version → fail-closed', but without a named constant for the maximum known version, implementers must hard-code an integer comparison or infer the rule from the record example. This is a processing decision rule that is not co-located with the schema it governs.
findings.location: What section — 'The record' and 'Four record states'; Scope — 'New module under src/rebar/_store/'
findings.suggested_fix: Export `CURRENT_FORMAT_VERSION: int = 1` from the new module alongside `KNOWN_CAPABILITIES`. The gate reads: `if record['format_version'] > CURRENT_FORMAT_VERSION: raise StoreIncompatibleError(...)`. AC1 should reference this constant by name.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
